questions.question: What does the Dietary Inflammatory Index (DII) measure?

options.text: The total calorie content of your diet
How many servings of fruits and vegetables you eat per day
The overall inflammatory potential of your entire diet based on 45 food parameters
The amount of protein in your diet relative to carbohydrates

questions.explanation: The DII is a literature-derived scoring tool that rates the inflammatory potential of your entire diet based on 45 food parameters. Negative scores indicate anti-inflammatory diets, scores near zero are neutral, and positive scores indicate pro-inflammatory diets. It was developed by reviewing nearly 2,000 peer-reviewed studies.

questions.question: According to the NHANES analysis of 70,190 participants, people with the most pro-inflammatory diets had what increased odds of depression?

options.text: 12% higher odds
35% higher odds
55% higher odds
82% higher odds

questions.explanation: The NHANES analysis of 70,190 participants found that people in the highest DII quartile (most pro-inflammatory diets) had 82% higher odds of depression compared to the lowest quartile. The relationship was present across genders, age groups, and racial/ethnic groups.

questions.question: Why is the dose-response relationship between DII scores and depression considered particularly strong evidence?

options.text: It means only people with the worst diets need to worry about depression
It means every incremental improvement in dietary inflammation is associated with a proportional reduction in depression risk
It proves that diet is the only cause of depression
It means you must achieve a perfect anti-inflammatory diet to see any benefit

questions.explanation: The dose-response relationship means each increment in dietary inflammation is associated with a proportional increase in depression risk — and conversely, every improvement helps. You do not need a perfect diet to benefit. This linear, proportional pattern is one of the strongest indicators of a causal relationship in epidemiology.

questions.question: Which dietary pattern consistently scores as the most anti-inflammatory in DII analyses?

options.text: A high-protein, zero-carbohydrate diet
A diet consisting entirely of supplements and meal replacement shakes
The Mediterranean diet — emphasizing olive oil, vegetables, fruits, fish, legumes, and nuts
A diet focused exclusively on organic meat and dairy

questions.explanation: The Mediterranean diet, with its emphasis on olive oil, vegetables, fruits, fish, legumes, nuts, and moderate wine, consistently scores as one of the most anti-inflammatory patterns in DII analyses. It aligns naturally with gut-brain supportive eating and is associated with lower depression risk.

questions.question: What is the recommended 'shift, don't switch' approach to lowering dietary inflammation?

options.text: Completely overhaul your diet overnight to eliminate all pro-inflammatory foods
Focus on adding anti-inflammatory foods gradually rather than dramatic elimination, since even modest improvements reduce depression risk
Only eat raw foods for 30 days to reset your inflammatory markers
Take anti-inflammatory supplements instead of changing your diet

questions.explanation: The 'shift, don't switch' approach recommends gradual additions of anti-inflammatory foods rather than overnight overhauls. Research shows that even modest improvements in DII score are associated with reduced depression risk. When you add more anti-inflammatory foods, they naturally displace some pro-inflammatory ones without the psychological burden of deprivation.

title: Dietary Inflammation and the DII Score - Quiz
